Results.Query: What is the capital of France?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The user is asking about the capital of France. This is a simple, well-known fact that doesn't require a Wikipedia search. The capital of France is Paris.

The capital of France is **Paris**! 🗼

Paris is not only the political capital but also the cultural, economic, and historical heart of France. It is home to iconic landmarks such as the Eiffel Tower, the Louvre Museum, and Notre-Dame Cathedral, and it serves as the seat of the French government.

Results.Query: Write an apology letter to a friend whose birthday I missed
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The user wants help writing an apology letter for missing a friend's birthday. This is a creative writing task, not a factual question that requires Wikipedia research. I can handle this directly without using any tools.
</thinking>

Here's a heartfelt apology letter you can personalize for your friend:

---

**Subject: I'm So Sorry I Missed Your Birthday 🎂**

Dear [Friend's Name],

I hope this letter finds you well. I'm writing because I owe you a sincere and long-overdue apology — **I missed your birthday**, and that is completely unacceptable given how much you mean to me.

There is no real excuse for it. Whether I got caught up in the busyness of life or simply lost track of the date, the bottom line is that I let you down on a day that was supposed to be all about celebrating *you*. You deserve so much better than that, and I am truly sorry.

You have always been there for me — through the good times and the tough ones — and the least I could do was acknowledge your special day. Missing it made me realize just how much I take your presence in my life for granted, and that is something I genuinely want to change.

I'd love to make it up to you. How about we plan a **belated birthday celebration** — just the two of us (or with the whole crew, whatever you prefer)? Dinner, a fun outing, or simply a good catch-up over coffee — I'm fully on board with whatever makes you smile. Consider it your very own "Part Two" birthday! 🎉

Again, I am deeply sorry. Your friendship is one of the things I treasure most, and I promise to do better going forward.

With love and a big (overdue) birthday wish,
**[Your Name]**

---

> 💡 **Tips to personalize this letter:**
> - Add a specific memory you share with your friend to make it more heartfelt.
> - Mention something special about them or why their friendship matters to you.
> - Include a birthday wish or a small gift alongside the letter for extra thoughtfulness.
> - Adjust the tone (more casual or more formal) based on your relationship.

Results.Query: Who was the first president of the United States?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The user is asking about the first president of the United States. This is a simple, well-known historical fact — George Washington. However, per my instructions, I should use Wikipedia for facts that must have references. Let me search for this.

Actually, the instructions say "Do not use wikipedia for creative, mathematical asks and simple facts such as what is the color of the sky." The first president of the US is a very simple, well-known fact. I can answer this without searching Wikipedia.
</thinking>

The first president of the United States was **George Washington**. He served two terms from **1789 to 1797**. Washington was a Founding Father, commander of the Continental Army during the American Revolutionary War, and is often referred to as the "Father of His Country." He was unanimously elected by the Electoral College, a distinction no other president has achieved.

Results.Query: When was the Eiffel Tower built?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>

The **Eiffel Tower** was constructed from **1887 to 1889**. It is located in Paris, France, and is named after the engineer **Gustave Eiffel**, whose company designed and built it. It was originally built for the **1889 World's Fair**. [Eiffel Tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eiffel_Tower)

Based on Wikipedia, **Tokyo** is the capital and most populous city of Japan. As of **2023**, Tokyo has a population of over **14 million people** (specifically **14,195,730**) in the city proper, representing about **11.5% of Japan's total population**. It's worth noting that the Greater Tokyo Area (including surrounding regions) is the most populous metropolitan area in the world, with tens of millions more residents. [Demographics of Tokyo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics_of_Tokyo)

Results.Query: What is the biggest country by land size?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The user is asking about the biggest country by land size. This is a simple, well-known fact — Russia is the largest country by land area. I don't need to search Wikipedia for this basic fact.
</thinking>

The largest country in the world by land area is **Russia**! 🌍

Russia covers approximately **17.1 million square kilometers (6.6 million square miles)**, making it by far the largest country on Earth. It spans across both Europe and Asia, covering more than one-eighth of Earth's total inhabited land area.

Here's a quick look at the **Top 5 largest countries by land area**:

| Rank | Country | Area (km²) |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | 🇷🇺 Russia | ~17,098,242 |
| 2 | 🇨🇦 Canada | ~9,984,670 |
| 3 | 🇺🇸 United States | ~9,833,517 |
| 4 | 🇨🇳 China | ~9,596,960 |
| 5 | 🇧🇷 Brazil | ~8,515,767 |

Russia is nearly **twice the size** of the second-largest country, Canada! 🏔️

Results.Query: What year did World War II end?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The search results clearly confirm the end of World War II. Let me compile the answer with citations.
</thinking>

World War II ended in **1945**, though it concluded on two separate fronts:

- **In Europe**, the war ended in **May 1945**, following the suicide of Adolf Hitler on April 30 and the subsequent surrender of Nazi Germany. [End of World War II in Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe)

- **In Asia**, the war officially ended on **September 2, 1945**, when Japan formally surrendered aboard the USS Missouri. [End of World War II in Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Asia)

---

**References:**
1. [End of World War II in Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe)
2. [End of World War II in Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Asia)

Results.Query: Who invented the telephone?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The search results clearly indicate that Alexander Graham Bell is credited with patenting the first practical telephone. Let me provide a well-cited answer.
</thinking>

## The Invention of the Telephone

**Alexander Graham Bell** is widely credited with inventing the telephone. He was a Scottish-born inventor, scientist, and engineer who is recognized for **patenting the first practical telephone**. He also went on to co-found the **American Telephone and Telegraph Company (AT&T)**. [Alexander Graham Bell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell)

It's worth noting that the invention of the telephone has been a subject of historical and legal debate, with other inventors such as **Elisha Gray** and **Antonio Meucci** also being recognized for their contributions to its development. [Telephone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telephone) | [The Telephone Cases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Telephone_Cases)

---

- [Telephone – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telephone)

Results.Query: What is the speed of light?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The speed of light is a simple, well-known scientific fact that doesn't require a Wikipedia search. I can answer this directly.
</thinking>

The speed of light in a vacuum is **299,792,458 meters per second** (approximately **3 × 10⁸ m/s**, or about **186,282 miles per second**).

This is denoted by the letter **c** and is one of the fundamental constants of the universe. According to Einstein's theory of special relativity, the speed of light is the ultimate speed limit — nothing with mass can reach or exceed it.

Results.Query: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
Results.ActualAnswer:
The **Mona Lisa** was painted by the Italian Renaissance artist **Leonardo da Vinci**. According to [Mona Lisa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mona_Lisa), it is believed to have been painted between **1503 and 1506**, though Leonardo may have continued working on it as late as **1517**.

Results.Query: Who built the Space Needle?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
Now I have good information. Let me compile the details:

1. The Space Needle was conceived by Edward E. Carlson, chairman of the 1962 World's Fair in Seattle.
2. The architect was John Graham Jr. (of the firm John Graham & Company).
3. The general contractor/builder was Howard S. Wright Companies.

Let me provide a comprehensive answer with citations.
</thinking>

The **Space Needle** was the result of a collaboration between several key individuals and companies:

- **Conceived by Edward E. Carlson**: The idea originated with Edward E. Carlson, chairman of the 1962 World's Fair (Century 21 Exposition) in Seattle. [Space Needle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle)

- **Designed by architect John Graham Jr.**: The architectural firm **John Graham & Company** was responsible for the design of the tower. [Space Needle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle)

- **Built by Howard S. Wright Companies**: The general contractor who physically constructed the Space Needle was **Howard S. Wright Construction Company**. [Howard S. Wright Companies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_S._Wright_Companies)

The Space Needle was built for the **1962 World's Fair** in Seattle, Washington, and has since become an iconic landmark and observation tower in the city. [Space Needle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle)

---

Results.Query: What is the largest desert in the world?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
Now I have enough information. According to the Wikipedia article on the Sahara, it is the "largest hot desert in the world and the third-largest desert overall, smaller only than the deserts of Antarctica and the northern Arctic." This confirms that the Antarctic Desert is the largest desert in the world, followed by the Arctic Desert, and then the Sahara.

The Antarctica article states it has an area of 14,200,000 km² (5,500,000 sq mi).

Let me compile this information into a clear answer.
</thinking>

The **Antarctic Desert** is the largest desert in the world! 🌍

While many people assume the Sahara holds this title, it is actually only the **largest *hot* desert** in the world and the **third-largest desert overall**. According to Wikipedia, the Sahara is *"smaller only than the deserts of Antarctica and the northern Arctic."* [Sahara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahara)

Here's a quick breakdown:

| Rank | Desert | Type | Area |
|------|--------|------|------|
| 1 | **Antarctic Desert** | Polar (Cold) | ~14,200,000 km² |
| 2 | **Arctic Desert** | Polar (Cold) | ~13,900,000 km² |
| 3 | **Sahara** | Hot | ~9,000,000 km² |

Antarctica, covering an area of **14,200,000 km² (5,500,000 sq mi)**, qualifies as a desert because it receives extremely little precipitation — making it the coldest, driest, and windiest continent on Earth. [Antarctica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctica)

So, the key distinction is:
- **Largest desert overall** → 🧊 **Antarctic Desert** (polar/cold desert)
- **Largest hot desert** → 🌞 **Sahara Desert**

---

### References
- [Sahara – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sahara)
- [Antarctica – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctica)
- [List of Deserts – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_deserts)
FailureReasons: None of the expected URLs were referenced: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctic_Desert, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_deserts_by_area

Results.Query: Tell me about Mercury
Tell me about Python
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer about both Python the programming language and Pythons the snake. Let me compile the information and cite the relevant sources.
</thinking>

Great question! "Python" can refer to two very different things — a **programming language** and a **family of snakes**. Here's a look at both:

---

# 🐍 Python (the Snake)

Pythons are a fascinating group of reptiles. According to [Pythonidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pythonidae), **Pythonidae** — commonly known as pythons — are a **family of nonvenomous snakes** found in **Africa, Asia, and Australia**. Among their members are some of the **largest snakes in the world**. Key facts include:

- They are **constrictors**, meaning they subdue prey by squeezing rather than using venom.
- They are found across diverse habitats including rainforests, grasslands, and deserts.
- Well-known species include the **Reticulated Python**, **Burmese Python**, and **Ball Python**.
- They are **ectothermic** (cold-blooded) reptiles, regulating body temperature through their environment.

---

# 💻 Python (the Programming Language)

Python is one of the most popular programming languages in the world today. According to [Python (programming language)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_(programming_language)):

- Python is a **high-level, general-purpose programming language** that emphasizes **code readability, simplicity, and ease of writing**.
- It supports **multiple programming paradigms**, with an emphasis on **object-oriented programming** and **dynamic typing**.
- It was conceived in the **late 1980s** and its implementation was started in **December 1989** by **Guido van Rossum** at CWI (Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica) in the Netherlands, as noted by [History of Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Python).
- It was **first publicly released in 1991**.
- [Guido van Rossum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guido_van_Rossum) (born 31 January 1956) is a Dutch programmer and is the **creator** of Python. He is often referred to as Python's "Benevolent Dictator For Life" (BDFL).

### 🔑 Key Features of Python:
| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Readability** | Clean, English-like syntax |
| **Versatility** | Used in web dev, AI/ML, data science, automation, and more |
| **Dynamic Typing** | Variable types are determined at runtime |
| **Large Ecosystem** | Thousands of libraries (e.g., NumPy, TensorFlow, Django) |
| **Open Source** | Free to use and distribute |

### 🏛️ Why Is It Called "Python"?
Interestingly, the name "Python" was **not** inspired by the snake — it was inspired by the British comedy series **Monty Python's Flying Circus**, which van Rossum was a fan of.

---

## Which Python Were You Asking About?
Let me know if you'd like to dive deeper into either the **programming language** or the **snake**! 😊

---

## References
- [Pythonidae – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pythonidae)

- [History of Python – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Python)
- [Guido van Rossum – Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guido_van_Rossum)
